evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns and that are addictive by design.
in games that are targeted to kids with gacha systems (lotteries, slots, etc.)
certain influencers are not only walking advertisers of certains games

paragraphs.text: I had my experiences with games and apps that are related to dark patterns and that are addictive by design. Not only the infinite scroll down who favours the so called "doom-scrolling" but also in games that are targeted to kids with gacha systems (lotteries, slots, etc.) who focus on conditioning and use techniques similar to those of a casino. Then you have thinks like login bonus or weekly bonus, etc. that favour that you become an app or a game part of your daily routine making it harder to escape from it, as it fosters that you play everyday, or else you will lose progression, which happens not only in mobile games but also in console games as well. I do believe a regulation onn dark patterns needs to be enforced at the level of the EU, not only because of how this practices take advantage of psychology studies to make it more addictive or part of your daily routine but also because they target people indiscriminately and they tend to cater to your spedning habits (if you spend more, they give you more "offers"), personalizing them. It doesn't help that certain influencers are not only walking advertisers of certains games, but a few of them also promote the so called "skin casinos" using items that you can get in a game for free, or by paying a penny for a loot box (randomized content) and then using it as a casino chip so you can try and get things more pricey and exclusive... or no and fail. Things like "CS:GO Lotto" follow a grey area and promote the possibility of minors gambling with things that, while they are not money, they can be bought with it or even exchanged for money. All in all I think we should be harsh in the regulation with these types of game at a Union level and follow the path that other countries (not only in the EU, like Netherlands, but other countries like China, which legislate about these too), and create a law that could be protective for everyone and specifically, of the minors
